Trials should be designed to maximize inclusiveness and broad representative enrollment.

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
The Oncology Guidelines® are a statement of evidence and consensus of the authors regarding their views of currently accepted approaches to 
treatment. Any clinician seeking to apply or consult the Oncology Guidelines is expected to use independent medical judgment in the context of individual 
clinical circumstances to determine any patient’s care or treatment. The Oncology Guidelines® (Oncology Guidelines®) makes no representations 
or warranties of any kind regarding their content, use or application and disclaims any responsibility for their application or use in any way. The Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines are copyrighted by Oncology Guidelines®. All rights reserved. The Oncology Guidelines and the illustrations herein may not 
be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines. ©2026.
Oncology Guidelines Soft Tissue Sarcoma Panel Members
Summary of the Guidelines Updates
Soft Tissue Sarcoma
• Extremity/Body Wall, Head/Neck (EXTSARC-1)
• Retroperitoneal/Intra-Abdominal (RETSARC-1)
• Desmoid Tumors (Aggressive Fibromatosis) (DESM-1)
• Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS-1)
• Atypical Lipomatous Tumor and Well-Differentiated Liposarcoma (ALT/WDLPS-1)
• Borderline Phyllodes Tumor of the Breast (PHYLLSARC-1)
• Malignant Phyllodes Tumor of the Breast (PHYLLSARC-2)
Principles of Imaging (SARC-A)
Principles of Pathologic Assessment of Sarcoma Specimens (SARC-B)
Principles of Ancillary Techniques Useful in the Diagnosis of Sarcomas (SARC-C)
Principles of Surgery (SARC-D)
Principles of Radiation Therapy (SARC-E)
Principles of Interventional Oncology (SARC-F)
Systemic Therapy Agents and Regimens with Activity in Soft Tissue Sarcoma Subtypes 
and Aggressive Soft Tissue Neoplasms (SARC-G)
Principles of Cancer Risk Assessment and Counseling (SARC-H)

Bone Sarcomas - Oncology Guidelines for Bone Cancer
Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumors - Oncology Guidelines for Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumors 
Uterine Sarcomas - Oncology Guidelines for Uterine Neoplasms

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma include:
UPDATES
Continued
Global
• References for systemic therapy agents and regimens were updated 
throughout the guidelines.
EXTSARC-1
Work Up /Essential
• Bullet 6, new: Assess for Distress, with the following footnote: Refer 
to the Oncology Guidelines for Distress Thermometer and Problem List, 
which includes social determinants of health. See Oncology Guidelines for 
Distress.
Useful in Certain Circumstances
• Deleted footnote: Different subtypes have different propensities to spread 
to various locations.
Special considerations for unique histologies
• Added: RETSARC-2 after Ewing Sarcoma.
• New algorithm: Benign phyllodes, linking to the Oncology Guidelines Breast Cancer 
Guidelines.
• New algorithm: Borderline/Malignant phyllodes, linking to PHYLLSARC-1 
and PHYLLSARC-2.
EXTSARC-2
• Page Title modified to include: Multimodality Treatment is Critical, (also 
for EXTSARC-4).
• Column 1: added "or" after Stage 1A.
Footnotes
• j, revised: See Principles of Surgery (SARC-D) including Surgery can
often result in physical disability. See guidance for prehabilitation and 
rehabilitation (SARC-D 3 of 4). (Also for EXTSARC-3A).
• New footnote: In the rare situation where surgery is not an option, 
consider observation.
EXTSARC-3 
• Column 3, Stage III, new footnote: In select histologies and locations 
there may be a role for neoadjuvant pembrolizumab (SARC-G), 
corresponding to neoadjuvant systemic therapy + RT.
Last row, footnote added: Fungating necrotic bleeding and/or super 
infected tumors may not allow for pre-operative radiation and/or 
neoadjuvant chemotherapy and may be better served with surgery 
followed by adjuvant therapy.
• Column 5, neoadjuvant systemic therapy, surgery to obtain oncologically 
appropriate margins: RT added category 1 designation.
• Column 6, bullet 1, modified: H&P imaging every 3-6...
Sub-bullet 1, modified: Chest Imaging for metastatic disease.
Treatment
• New footnote: Owen D, Harmsen WS, Ahmed SK, et al. Highs and Lows 
of Spatially Fractionated Radiation Therapy: Dosimetry and Clinical 
Outcomes. Pract Radiat Oncol, 2025;15:e388-e395 corresponding to 
palliative options.
• New bullet: Resection of involved lymph nodes/regional nodal dissection 
for nodal involvement ± RT ± systemic therapy.
SARC-G, 1 of 14
Subsequent Lines of Therapy for Advanced/Metastatic Disease
Other Recommended
• Modified: Ifosfamide/Mesna
Useful in Certain Circumstances
• Erratum: Revised regimen from pembrolizumab or ipilimumab + nivolumab to "pembrolizumab or nivolumab ± ipilimumab"
Updates in Version 3.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma include:
Updates in Version 2.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma include:
SARC-G, 4 of 14
• Epithelioid Sarcoma: Tazemetostat removed.
Footnote, removed: Single-agent therapy for the treatment of metastatic or locally advanced epithelioid sarcoma not eligible for complete resection.
 ◊Reference, removed: Stacchiotti S, Schoffski P, Jones R, et al. Safety and efficacy of tazemetostat, a first-in-class EZH2 inhibitor, in patients with 
epithelioid sarcoma (NCT0261950). JClin Oncol 2019;37:11003.

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
Continued
RETSARC-2
• Column 1, modified: ... Resectable primary disease.
Footnote
• n, modified: Consider systemic therapy if high risk for metastatic disease or if downstaging is needed to facilitate resection. Systemic therapy is not 
recommended for low-grade tumors such as well-differentiated liposarcoma with no evidence of dedifferentiation. (Also for RETSARC-6)
RETSARC-3
• R2, Column 2, modified: Adjuvant RT should not be administered routinely with the exception of highly selected patients and unless progressive disease 
local recurrence would cause undue morbidity.
DESM-2
Footnotes 
• Deleted: For tumors that are symptomatic, or impairing or threatening in function, patients should be offered therapy with the decision based on the 
location of the tumor and potential morbidity of the therapeutic option. (Also for DESM-3).
• e, modified: Spontaneous regression has been reported in up to 20% of patients, supporting an initial period of observation in patients with newly 
diagnosed desmoid tumors (Gounder MM, et al. N Engl J Med 2023;388:898-912; Kasper B, et al. Future Oncol 2025;21:889-901). 2018;379:2417-
2428. (Also for DESM-3). 
DESM-4
• Column 4, R0: deleted: Adjuvant RT (category 3).
R1, deleted: Adjuvant RT (category 2B).
R2, modified: Ablation/embolization
 ◊Deleted: procedures.
Footnote
• Deleted: Consider RT for lesions where recurrence would be technically challenging to resect and would lead to significant morbidity.
RMS-1
Treatment
• Column 3, modified: Recommend treating like high-grade Soft Tissue Sarcoma undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) (see SARC-G for systemic 
therapy options).
PHYLLSARC-1
• Phyllodes tumor of the breast is an new section.
SARC-A
Principles of Imaging
Global: 
• FDG-PET/CT has been modified to FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI).
• Bullet 1, modified: Plain radiographs For long-term survivors, chest x-rays and nonionizing imaging modalities may be considered to minimize radiation 
exposure or MRI may be substituted for long-term survivors on surveillance to minimize radiation exposure from CT imaging. Consider FDG-PET/Fused 
3-dimensional imaging in staging, prognostication, grading, and determining response to chemotherapy with the following footnote: FDG-PET/CT is 
more common but FDG-PET/MRI can be considered depending on availability and physician's opinion.
UPDATES
Continued
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma include:

Version 3.2026, 03/12/26 © 2026 Oncology Guidelines® (Oncology Guidelines®), All rights reserved. Oncology Guidelines® and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
Continued
SARC-A (continued)
Principles of Imaging
Table 1
• The table has been divided.
• Column 3, row, 2, modified: MRI (preferred for extremity/body wall, head and neck and cardiac) and/or CT (preferred for retroperitoneum).
• Column 4, all rows, deleted, As part of ... at the beginning of the sentence and Added "For".
SARC-A, 5 of 5
Evaluation and Workup
• New: CT or MRI Contrasted cross-sectional imaging with MRI (with and without contrast) is preferred over CT (with contrast, unless otherwise stated). 
(Also for Surveillance).
• Biopsy should be performed for suspicious masses to confirm the diagnosis
May not be necessary if complete resection is planned SARC-B
Principles of Pathologic Assessment of Sarcoma Specimens
• Sub-bullet 9, Sub, sub-bullet 5, new sentence: For patients who have received neoadjuvant therapy, information on treatment response and viable 
tumor.
SARC-C, 1 of 4
• Principles of Ancillary Techniques Useful in the Diagnosis of Sarcomas
• Footnote 2, sentence 2 deleted: For example, additional genetic aberrations can be found in alveolar RMS, including PAX3::NCOA1, PAX3::NCOA2, 
and PAX3::INO80D. NCOA2 gene rearrangements and MYOD mutation have been identified in spindle cell RMS. Receptor tyrosine kinase/RAS/
PIK3CA aberrations are found in 93% of RMS cases. MIR143::NOTCH fusion has recently been identified in glomus tumor. Loss of TSC1 (9q34) or 
TSC2 (16p13.3) (mTOR pathway) or gene fusions of the TFE3 gene (microphthalmia-associated transcription factor family) have been identified in 
perivascular epithelioid cell tumor (PEComa).
SARC-C, 4 of 4
• Deleted, Leiomyosarcoma (LMS) under Tumor, Complete alterations under Aberration, Unknown under Gene(s) involved.
SARC-D, 1 of 4
• Principles of Surgery
Modified: The multidisciplinary surgical team should include expertise in sarcoma with the management of sarcomas at specific anatomic sites as well 
as reconstruction. management including plastic, reconstructive, vascular and thoracic surgeons is recommended.  
• Biopsy
Bullet 4, new: There is no clarity in the literature on when sentinel node staging in STS should be done. A multidisciplinary discussion should 
occur regarding its role in certain histologies with a possibility of occult lymph node metastasis (eg, clear cell, angiosarcoma, epithelioid, alveolar 
rhabdomyosarcoma) during treatment planning. 
• Deleted: For certain histologies with a propensity for nodal metastatic disease, sentinel node biopsy can be considered, especially if the presence of 
occult nodal metastatic disease would change the multimodality treatment plan.

Version 3.2026, 03/12/26 © 2026 Oncology Guidelines® (Oncology Guidelines®), All rights reserved. Oncology Guidelines® and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
Continued
SARC-D, 2 of 4
• Failure to Obtain Oncologically Appropriate Margins: Added, Extremity, Truncal, Head/Neck.
• Failure to Obtain Oncologically Appropriate Margins: Added, Retroperitoneal/Intra-abdominal.
SARC-E, 1 of 6
• Principles of Radiation Therapy
Bullet 4, 2nd sentence: Added (preferred) to IMRT. 
SARC-F 2 of 3
Principles of Interventional Oncology
Ablation
• Bullet 1,modified: Tumor ablation involves application of FDA-approved thermal or non-thermal device therapies to a tumor to achieve cell death. 
Thermal ablation achieves tissue destruction by the induction of extreme hypothermia (cryoablation) or hyperthermia (radiofrequency ablation [RFA], 
microwave ablation, laser ablation, and high-intensity focused ultrasound [HIFU]). Non-thermal ablation such as irreversible electroporation (IRE) 
results in permanent cellular membrane injury. 
• Bullet 2, new: Tumor ablation techniques include thermal and non-thermal approaches.
Sub-bullet 1, modified: Ablation modality should be based on tumor size, location, and adjacent critical structures to optimize treatment effect while 
limiting potential adverse events. Choice of treatment modality may also take into account institutional experience and technique availability.
Deleted: Ablation can include the target lesion in addition to a margin of radiologically normal tissue to ensure complete local treatment. 
Deleted: Adjunct passive and active thermoprotective techniques, such as hydrodissection, could be used to protect adjacent critical structures during 
percutaneous ablation. 
• Bullet 5, sub-bullet 2 modified: Cryoablation is the recommended most commonly utilized ablation modality.
SARC-G, 1 of 14
Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Therapy
Preferred
• Bullet 3, new: Neoadjuvant Pembrolizumab with RT followed by adjuvant Pembrolizumab for UPS related sarcomas (limb girdle/extremity UPS/
myxofibrosarcoma [MFS]/dedifferentiated liposarcoma [ddLPS]/pleomorphic liposarcoma)
First-Line Therapy Advanced/Metastatic
Useful in Certain Circumstances
• Deleted: MAID (Mesna/Doxorubicin/Ifosfamide/Dacarbazine) (Also for DFSP, Other recommended, on SARC-G 4 of 14 and solitary Fibrous Tumor, 
Other recommended on SARC-G 6 of 14).
SARC-G,  3 of 14
Alveolar Soft Part Sarcoma (ASPS)
Preferred
• Bullet 4, new: Axitinib + Pembrolizumab
SARC-G 8 of 14 
Footnotes
• New: Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab. Pembrolizumab and 
berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV pembrolizumab.

Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
EXTSARC-1

ESSENTIAL:
• Prior to the initiation of therapy, all patients should be evaluated 
and treated by a multidisciplinary team with expertise and 
experience in sarcomaa 
• History and physical (H&P)
• Adequate imaging of primary tumorb is indicated for all lesions 
with a reasonable chance of being malignant
• Carefully planned core needle [preferred] or incisional biopsy 
should be performed after adequate imaging (SARC-D)
Place biopsy along future resection axis with minimal 
dissection and careful attention to hemostasis
Biopsy should establish grade and histologic subtypec
As appropriate, use ancillary diagnostic methodologiesd 
• Obtain imaging of potential sites of metastatic disease 
• Assess for distresse
USEFUL UNDER CERTAIN CIRCUMSTANCES:
• Additional imaging as indicated; see Principles of Imaging 
(SARC-A)
• The following conditions are linked to increased incidence of 
sarcoma and other cancers:
For patients with neurofibromatosis,f see Oncology Guidelines for 
Central Nervous System Cancers (PSCT-3) 
 ◊For pheochromocytoma, see Oncology Guidelines for 
Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors
For Li-Fraumeni syndrome, see Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/
Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, 
and Prostate
For hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC or 
Lynch syndrome), see Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial 
High-Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric
For patients with personal/family history suggestive of other 
cancer predisposition syndromes, consider further genetics 
assessment

Oncology Guidelines Version 3.2026
Extremity/Body Wall, Head/Neck
Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
FOOTNOTES
a	These guidelines are intended to treat the adult population. For adolescent and young adult patients, see Oncology Guidelines for Adolescent and Young Adult (AYA) 
Oncology. 
b	Imaging studies should include cross-sectional imaging (MRI with and without contrast and/or CT with contrast) to provide details about the size of tumor and contiguity 
to nearby visceral structures and neurovascular landmarks. Other imaging studies such as angiogram and plain x-ray may be warranted in selected circumstances. 
See Principles of Imaging (SARC-A). 
c Principles of Pathologic Assessment of Sarcoma Specimens (SARC-B).
d Principles of Ancillary Techniques Useful in the Diagnosis of Sarcomas (SARC-C).
e Refer to the Oncology Guidelines for Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. See Oncology Guidelines for Distress 
Management (DIS-A).
f Patients with neurofibromatosis are at risk for multiple sarcomas at various locations and their assessment and follow-up should be different (Reilly KM, et al. J Natl 
Cancer Inst 2017;109:djx124). Consider FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) for initial workup to differentiate between neurofibroma(s) and malignant peripheral nerve 
sheath tumor (MPNST).
g Diagnoses that will impact the overall treatment plan. See SARC-G for special considerations for unique histologies.
h Patients with DFSP with fibrosarcomatous changes and/or malignant transformations should be treated according to the algorithms for other soft tissue sarcomas of 
the extremity/body wall and head/neck (EXTSARC-1 and SARC-G, 4 of 14).
EXTSARC-1A

Discussion
EXTSARC-2
i See American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) Staging, 8th Edition (ST-5 and ST-6). 
j See Principles of Surgery (SARC-D) including guidance for prehabilitation and rehabilitation (SARC-D 3 of 4).
kSee SARC-D for either a previous unplanned excision or a failure to obtain oncologically appropriate margins with planned sarcoma surgery.
l In the rare situation where surgery is not an option, consider observation.
m Principles of Imaging (SARC-A).
n In situations where the area is easily followed by physical examination, imaging may not be required.
o After 10 years, the likelihood of developing a recurrence is small and follow-up should be individualized.
PRIMARY TREATMENT  
(MULTIMODALITY TREATMENT IS CRITICAL)
FOLLOW-UP
Stage IAi or 
Stage IBi (low grade)
• H&P every 3–6 mo for 3 y, 
then annually
• Obtain baseline imagingm 
see SARC-A
• Periodic imaging of primary 
sitem based upon risk of 
locoregional recurrencen,o
• Consider chest imagingm
If recurrence, see 
Recurrent Disease
(EXTSARC-6)
• Surgeryj,k to obtain 
oncologically  
appropriate marginsl

management of nodal disease.
q See Principles of Radiation Therapy (SARC-E) for discussion of adjuvant versus neoadjuvant therapy.
r In selected cases when margin status is uncertain, consultation with a radiation oncologist is recommended.
s In select histologies and locations, there may be a role for neoadjuvant pembrolizumab (SARC-G).
t Fungating necrotic bleeding and/or super infected tumors may not allow for preoperative radiation and/or neoadjuvant chemotherapy and may be better served with surgery 
followed by adjuvant therapy.
u Systemic Therapy Agents and Regimens with Activity in Soft Tissue Sarcoma Subtypes and Aggressive Soft Tissue Neoplasms (SARC-G).
v Neoadjuvant-based chemotherapy may be beneficial in patients with chemotherapy-sensitive tumor types with high-probability of mortality from sarcoma based on available 
nomograms, FNCLCC Grade 3 sarcomas, or in cases where downsizing tumor would facilitate complete resection. (Spunt SL, et al. Lancet Oncol 2020;21:145-161; Pasquali S, 
et al. Eur J Cancer 2019;109:51-60; Pasquali S, et al. Cancer 2022;128:85-93; Italiano A, et al. Ann Oncol 2010;21:2436-2441).
w FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) may be useful in determining response to systemic therapy (Schuetze SM, et al. Cancer 2005;103:339-348).
x A prospective study demonstrated low rates of local recurrence with surgery alone in carefully selected patients with high-grade tumors <5 cm (Pisters PW, et al. Ann Surg 
2007;246:675-681). Consider omission of RT for tumors <5 cm resected with wide margins if a repeat resection would be feasible with low morbidity in the case of a recurrence.
y Observation alone can be considered following a resection with a widely negative margin if the risk for local recurrence is very low.

disease (primarily lung); the ultimate choice of local control modality may depend 
on factors such as performance status, patient preference, lesion location/
accessibility, ability to preserve normal tissue function, and anticipated morbidity 
of a treatment modality.

be effective for treatment of sarcoma metastases. Dose and fractionation should 
be determined by an experienced radiation oncologist based on normal tissue 
constraints (Dhakal S, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2012;82:940-945; 
Navarria P, et al. Eur J Cancer 2015;51:668-674; Baumann BC, et al. J Surg 
Oncol 2020;122:877-883).

on factors such as performance status, patient preference, lesion location/
accessibility, ability to preserve normal tissue function, and anticipated morbidity 
of a treatment modality. 
cc In retrospective studies, various SBRT dosing regimens have been reported to 
be effective for treatment of sarcoma metastases. Dose and fractionation should 
be determined by an experienced radiation oncologist based on normal tissue 
constraints (Dhakal S, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2012;82:940-945;

case basis whether re-irradiation is possible. Some case series suggest benefit 
with re-irradiation (Catton C, et al. Radiother Oncol 1996;41:209-214) while 
others do not (Torres MA, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2007;67:1124-1129), 
likely reflecting differences in selection of patients for treatment with surgery and 
radiotherapy or surgery alone. Brachytherapy, intensity-modulated RT (IMRT), and/
or proton beam therapy may be utilized to reduce the morbidity of re-irradiation.
gg Based upon tumor biology and disease-free interval, consideration for additional 
chemotherapy and/or radiation (if previously administered) would be made on a 
case-by-case basis by a sarcoma multidisciplinary team.
hh Consider preoperative or postoperative radiation if radiation was not 
administered previously.

RMS-1
a RMS that is identified within another histology should be treated as the original histology. This pathway refers to patients diagnosed with pure RMS after full slide 
review.
b FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) scan may be useful for initial staging because of the possibility of nodal metastases and the appearance of unusual sites of initial 
metastatic disease in adult patients. See Principles of Imaging (SARC-A). 
c Referral to centers with expertise in the management of pediatric cancers is recommended.
d Not to be confused with anaplastic variant in children.
e Up to 13% of RMS in younger patients may have anaplastic features and should not be confused with the high-grade tumors seen in adults designated as pleomorphic 
RMS.
f Systemic therapy options for RMS may be different than those used with other soft tissue sarcoma histologies. See Systemic Therapy Agents and Regimens 
with Activity in Soft Tissue Sarcoma Subtypes and Aggressive Soft Tissue Neoplasms (SARC-G).

a ALT of the extremity, trunk, and abdominal wall is generally more indolent and an attempted marginal resection technique should be tailored to minimize surgical 
morbidity. En bloc resection with negative margins is generally sufficient to obtain long-term local control. 
b For patients with ALT/WDLPS, observation is recommended for focally positive margins if, in the event of recurrence, re-resection would not result in undue morbidity. 
RT is reserved for selected patients with recurrent or deeply infiltrative primary lesions with a risk of local recurrence, depending on the tumor location and patient’s 
age.
c Surveillance physical exams and imaging intervals should be adjusted to account for the potential for local recurrence. 
d Routine chest imaging for metastatic disease is not necessary. See Principles of Imaging (SARC-A).

Oncology Guidelines Version 3.2026
Malignant Phyllodes Tumor of the Breast
Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
PHYLLSARC-2A
a For risk criteria, see Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate. 
b For borderline or malignant phyllodes tumors, wide excision means excision with the intention of obtaining surgical margins ≥1 cm. Narrow surgical margins are 
associated with increased local recurrence risk, but are not an absolute indication for mastectomy when partial mastectomy fails to achieve a margin width ≥1 cm. 
Mastectomy may be needed to obtain these margins (considerations may include tumor and breast size). 
c Radiation therapy is not a substitute for negative margins and additional surgery should be pursued for positive margins.
d Following adequate margin resection, adjuvant RT can be deferred. However adjuvant RT should be considered in the setting of increased risk of local recurrence 
(eg, mitoses >30/10 hpf, size >10 cm, malignant heterologous elements). Scenarios include (but are not limited to) multiple recurrent phyllodes, and surgically narrow 
margins due to chest wall with reduced local recurrence threshold. See Principles of Radiation Therapy (SARC-E) for dose recommendations.
e Given propensity for lung/bone/visceral metastases, consider additional imaging with whole body FDG-PET/CT to evaluate metastatic disease, bone scan, or brain 
MRI as clinically indicated (Parkes A, et al. Breast Cancer Res Treat 2021;186:871-882; Mituś JW, et al. World J Surg 2016;40:323-328). See Principles of Imaging 
(SARC-A).
f Consider preoperative RT in marginally unresectable disease or when anticipated narrow margins despite radical resection. Effort should be made to expedite the 
initiation of preoperative RT when recommended by the multidisciplinary team as delays may impact outcomes in this rapidly growing tumor. Surgical re-evaluation 
should occur during and immediately after preoperative RT to reassess resectability.
g Principles of Radiation Therapy (SARC-E).
h Systemic Therapy Agents and Regimens with Activity in Soft Tissue Sarcoma Subtypes and Aggressive Soft Tissue Neoplasms (SARC-G).
i Data indicate that local recurrence is less likely to occur with mastectomy than breast conservation surgery. If patient has undergone breast conservation surgery, 
adjuvant RT is recommended regardless of margin status. Neron M, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2020;27:1818-1827; Bartles SAL, et al. Eur J Cancer 2024;201:113924.
j Principles of Surgery (SARC-D).
k Due to high rates of local recurrence with narrow or positive margins, attempts at nipple or skin sparing approach in close proximity to the tumor to facilitate 
reconstruction is discouraged. The goal should be to obtain widely negative margins even if that requires resection of extensive amounts of skin. 
l Consider systemic therapy if high risk for occult metastatic disease or high-risk features (eg, mitoses >30/10 hpf, size >10 cm, malignant heterologous elements). Use 
of systemic therapy should be discussed with a multidisciplinary team with expertise and experience in phyllodes tumor and considered after careful discussion with 
surgeon.

PHYLLSARC-4A
h Systemic Therapy Agents and Regimens with Activity in Soft Tissue Sarcoma Subtypes and Aggressive Soft Tissue Neoplasms (SARC-G).
m Principles of Imaging (SARC-A). 
n Consider symptom-driven brain MRI with and without contrast for 5 years for malignant phyllodes tumor. 
o The Panel endorses the College of American Pathologists Protocol for standardized pathology reporting for all phyllodes tumors (https://documents.cap.org/protocols/
Breast.Phyllodes_1.1.0.1.REL_CAPCP.pdf).
p Patients with lymph node involvement (including isolated regional nodal metastatic disease) should undergo regional lymph node dissection ± RT. 
q Metastasectomy is the historical standard for patients with oligometastatic disease (primarily lung); the ultimate choice of local control modality may depend on 
factors such as performance status, patient preference, lesion location/accessibility, ability to preserve normal tissue function, and anticipated morbidity of a treatment 
modality. In a multicenter retrospective study of 51 patients with metastatic malignant phyllodes tumors, multivariate analysis showed that surgery of metastasis was 
independently associated with overall survival (OS) (HR, 0.33; 95% Cl, 0.14–0.78) (Neron M, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2020;27:1693-1699).
r In retrospective studies, various SBRT dosing regimens have been reported to be effective for treatment of sarcoma metastases. Dose and fractionation should be 
determined by an experienced radiation oncologist based on normal tissue constraints (Dhakal S, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2012;82:940-945; Navarria P, et 
al. Eur J Cancer 2015;51:668-674; Baumann BC, et al. J Surg Oncol 2020;122:877-883).
s Principles of Interventional Oncology (SARC-F).

SARC-A 
3 OF 5
STS of extremity, superficial/trunk, or head and neck (refer to SARC-A for guidance on imaging of specific histologic subtypes)
The differential diagnosis of STS of the extremities includes benign lesions and other malignant entities.
Evaluation and Workup
• Imaging of the primary tumor 
MRI (preferred) or CT (less sensitive) 
CT angiogram and plain radiographs may be useful in certain circumstances 
• Staging includes chest imaging
CT chest without contrast (preferred) or plain radiographs (less sensitive)
Surveillance 
• Long-term follow-up
Consider MRI (preferred) or CT (less sensitive) when MR is contraindicated
• Consider ultrasound (US) to detect early local recurrence/small nodules <0.5 cm 
• Chest imaging is useful for the detection of asymptomatic distant recurrence while primary site imaging may only be useful for patients at high-risk of local recurrence
CT chest without contrast (preferred) or plain radiographs (less sensitive)
• MRI should be preferably used for surveillance of primary site. US may aid in problem solving: solid nodule versus seroma/hematoma formation, or very small nodules 
<0.5 cm
Some STS histologies and presentations have a unique metastatic pattern that would necessitate additional sites of staging/surveillance imaging, in addition to the 
recommended primary sites.
• Consider the following: 
For angiosarcoma
 ◊CNS MRI (or CT if MRI is contraindicated)
 ◊Consider CT or FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) to evaluate regional lymph 
node basin
 ◊Consider FDG-PET/CT or bone scan to evaluate bones
For cardiac sarcoma (left sided)
 ◊Consider CNS MRI (or CT if MRI is contraindicated)
For clear cell sarcoma
 ◊Consider CT or FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) to evaluate regional lymph 
node basin
 ◊Consider FDG-PET/CT or bone scan to evaluate bones
For epithelioid sarcoma
 ◊CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis
 ◊Consider CT or FDG-PET/CT to evaluate regional lymph node basin
For leiomyosarcoma
 ◊CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis
 ◊Consider FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) or bone scan to evaluate bones
For myxoid/round cell liposarcoma
 ◊CT or MRI abdomen/pelvis
 ◊MRI of the total spine
 ◊Consider WB MRI (preferred) to evaluate soft tissues 
 ◊If unavailable, consider FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI)
For rhabdomyosarcoma
 ◊CT or FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) to evaluate regional lymph node basin
 ◊Consider FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) or bone scan to evaluate bones

SARC-A 
4 OF 5
STS of extremity, superficial/trunk, or head and neck (continued)
Surveillance (continued)
• Follow-up schedule by disease stage
Higher grade/larger tumors have a higher risk of dissemination
Internal hemorrhage/capsular rupture may have a higher risk of dissemination as well
Surveillance is more rigorous, especially during the first 5 years post resection
 ◊After 10 years, follow-up should be individualized
• Stage I tumors
Consider chest imaging every 6–12 months
 ◊CT without contrast (preferred) or plain radiographs (less sensitive)
Postoperative baseline and periodic imaging of the primary tumor site is recommended based upon risk of locoregional recurrence
 ◊MRI (preferred) or CT (less sensitive)
• Stage II/III and synchronous stage IV disease
Postoperative reimaging to assess the primary tumor site and rule out metastatic disease
 ◊MRI (preferred) or CT (less sensitive)
Baseline and periodic imaging of the primary site are recommended based upon risk of locoregional recurrence
Imaging of the chest and other known sites of metastatic disease should be performed every 2–6 months for 2–3 years, then every 6 months to complete a total of 5 
years, and then annually
 ◊CT without contrast (preferred) or plain radiographs (less sensitive)
Retroperitoneal or intra-abdominal STS
• The differential diagnosis of retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal soft tissue mass includes malignant lesions (such as other sarcomas, GIST, lymphomas, or germ cell 
tumors), desmoids, and benign lesions. Workup and evaluation recommendations are similar to that of extremity sarcomas. Pre-resection biopsy is not necessary 
for all patients; however, confirmation of a sarcoma diagnosis (including histologic subtype) is required for patients being considered for neoadjuvant therapy. Image-
guided (CT or US) core needle biopsy is preferred over surgical biopsy and should be performed if neoadjuvant therapy is being considered or for suspicion of 
malignancy other than sarcoma. 
Evaluation and Workup
• CT with contrast is preferred, although MRI is useful in certain circumstances.
• Chest imaging should be performed for histologies that have the potential for lung metastases
CT without contrast (preferred) or plain radiographs (less sensitive)
• For unresectable or stage IV disease, follow-up imaging is recommended to assess treatment response (RETSARC-4).
Options include CT with contrast (preferred) or MRI
• Patients whose tumors become resectable following primary treatment should be managed as described for resectable disease (RETSARC-2).
Surveillance
• Follow-up imaging every 3–6 months for 3 years, then every 6 months to complete a total of 5 years, and then annually, following management of primary disease 
(RETSARC-3)
Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT (preferred) or MRI

Desmoid tumors (aggressive fibromatoses)
The differential diagnosis for desmoids depends on location; it includes other sarcomas, other malignant carcinomas, and benign lesions.
Evaluation and Workup
• Imaging of the primary site as clinically indicated
Contrasted cross-sectional imaging with MRI (with and without contrast) is preferred over CT (with contrast, unless otherwise stated).
Surveillance 
• Imaging every 3–6 months for 3 years and then every 6–12 months thereafter
Contrasted cross-sectional imaging with MRI (with and without contrast) is preferred over CT (with contrast, unless otherwise stated).
US may be considered for select locations (eg, abdominal wall) for long-term follow-up. US should be done by an ultrasonographer experienced in 
musculoskeletal disease2
• Disease progression or recurrence should be managed as described under primary treatment for resectable or unresectable disease
Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS)
• FDG-PET imaging (CT or MRI) can be useful in certain circumstances for initial staging due to the possibility of nodal metastases and the appearance 
of unusual sites of initial metastatic disease

header: PRINCIPLES OF PATHOLOGIC ASSESSMENT OF SARCOMA SPECIMENS1
content:
• Biopsy should establish malignancy, provide a specific diagnosis where possible, and provide a grade where appropriate or feasible, recognizing that limited biopsy 
material may underestimate grade.
• In patients without a definitive diagnosis following initial biopsy due to limited sampling size, repeat image-guided core needle biopsy or, if necessary, incisional biopsy 
should be considered to make a diagnosis. 
• Pathologic assessment of biopsies and resection specimens should be carried out by an experienced sarcoma pathologist.
• Morphologic diagnosis based on microscopic examination of histologic sections remains the gold standard for sarcoma diagnosis. However, since several ancillary 
techniques are useful in support of morphologic diagnosis (including immunohistochemistry [IHC], classical cytogenetics, and molecular genetic testing), sarcoma 
diagnosis should be carried out by pathologists who have access to these ancillary methods.2
• The pathologic assessment should include evaluation of the following features, all of which should be specifically addressed in the pathology report:
Organ, site, and operative procedure
Primary diagnosis (using standardized nomenclature, such as the WHO 
Classification of Tumors of Soft Tissue and Bone3)  
Size of tumor
Histologic grade (at the least, specify low or high grade if applicable); ideally, 
grade using the Fédération Nationale des Centres de Lutte Contre le Cancer 
(FNCLCC) Sarcoma Group, NCI system, or appropriate diagnosis-specific 
grading system if applicable
Necrosis
 ◊Present or absent
 ◊Approximate extent (percentage) 
Status of margins of excision
 ◊Uninvolved
 ◊Close (state which margins, the measured distance, and the nature of the 
adjacent tissue such as fascia)
 ◊Involved (state which margins) 
 ◊Cannot be assessed
Status of lymph nodes
 ◊Site
 ◊Number examined
 ◊Number positive
Results of ancillary studies2
◊Type of testing (ie, electron microscopy, IHC, molecular genetic analysis)
 ◊Where performed 
Additional tumor features of potential clinical value
 ◊Mitotic rate per 10 high-power fields (HPFs)
 ◊Presence or absence of vascular invasion
 ◊Character of tumor margin (well circumscribed or infiltrative) 
 ◊For some tumors, characterization of the inflammatory infiltrate by 
immunohistochemical or other ancillary studies may be helpful (type and 
extent). 
 ◊For patients who have received neoadjuvant therapy, information on 
treatment response and viable tumor
TNM stage (see ST-5 through ST-7)

header: PRINCIPLES OF ANCILLARY TECHNIQUES USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF SARCOMAS
content:
Morphologic diagnosis based on microscopic examination of histologic sections remains the gold standard for sarcoma diagnosis. However, several 
ancillary techniques are useful in support of morphologic diagnosis, including IHC, classical cytogenetics, electron microscopy, and molecular genetic 
testing. Molecular genetic testing has emerged as an ancillary testing approach since many sarcoma types harbor characteristic genetic aberrations, 
including single base pair substitutions, deletions and amplifications, and translocations. Molecular testing utilizes multiple techniques such as 
fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-based methods, or next-generation sequencing (NGS)-based methods 
(including DNA and RNA sequencing).1 The selection of the “best” technique depends on the individual tumor and clinical needs. NGS may be beneficial; 
the timing of when to perform NGS and for which patients must be evaluated individually. NGS findings can: determine patient eligibility for clinical trials, 
identify actionable mutations that may not have been targeted previously, and select patients who may benefit from immunotherapy. Thus, NGS may 
be appropriate for patients who may qualify for and who are interested in enrolling in a clinical trial or for patients with disease that is refractory or has 
progressed on standard therapies. NGS also may be helpful in certain histologies where NGS is likely to provide clinically actionable information. NGS 
should not replace expert pathology review, as NGS only rarely results in a diagnosis change following expert review. Technically successful NGS on 
bone biopsies requires use of decalcification agents, such as ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), that do not interfere with genomic testing. Each 
type of molecular testing is associated with test limitations and sources of false-negative results; if negative results are received when a molecular 
aberration clinically was expected, discussion with the testing lab is highly recommended as testing by another technique may be indicated. Selected 
recurrent genetic aberrations in sarcoma2,3 are listed on the next page.
1 Molecular genetic analysis involves highly complex test methods. None of the methods are absolutely sensitive or provide results that are absolutely specific; test 
results must always be interpreted in the context of the clinical and pathologic features of the case. Testing should therefore be carried out by a pathologist with 
expertise in sarcoma diagnosis and molecular diagnostic techniques.
2 This table is not exhaustive for either sarcomas with characteristic genetic changes or the genes involved. 
3 Similar translocations can be seen in very different diseases; eg, EWSR1::ATF1 can be seen in both angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma as well as clear cell sarcoma.

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
SARC-D 
1 OF 4

The multidisciplinary surgical team should include expertise in the management of sarcomas at specific anatomic sites as well as reconstruction.
Biopsy
• A pretreatment pathologic diagnosis, including histologic subtype and grade, is almost always necessary for the optimal treatment of a soft tissue sarcoma (surgical 
resection margin planning, a discussion of neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and/or radiation).
• Prior to amputation or limb-sparing surgery, Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation (PM&R) physician consultation should be offered to provide education about 
functional outcomes of the planned surgery, set adjuvant goals, and establish care for longitudinal follow-up. 
• Percutaneous core needle biopsy is preferred as it is associated with a low risk for biopsy-related complications. The biopsy tract should avoid potential tumor 
contamination of uninvolved anatomic compartments and, ideally, be in line with any future surgical resection incision. In certain situations, especially deep-seated tumors, 
image-guided needle biopsy can improve diagnostic accuracy (avoid necrotic nondiagnostic areas or surrounding normal tissues, and thoroughly sample heterogenous 
tumors). Open incisional biopsy can be considered if percutaneous core needle biopsies do not lead to an adequate diagnosis. 
In certain situations, MRI and FDG-PET/CT imaging obtained prior to biopsy may allow for targeting of enhancing/metabolically active components and less necrotic 
regions of the tumor.
Needle gauge and imaging modality should be determined based on lesion location and ability to adequately visualize target and non-target anatomy. 
• There is no clarity in the literature on when sentinel node staging in STS should be done. A multidisciplinary discussion should occur regarding its role in certain 
histologies with a possibility of occult lymph node metastasis (eg, clear cell, angiosarcoma, epithelioid, alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma) during treatment planning.2
Oncologically Appropriate Surgery
• The ideal surgical procedure necessary to resect the tumor with oncologically appropriate margins would have pathologically negative resection margins. However, 
planned close margins or even microscopically positive margins may be appropriate to preserve critical structures (eg, major vessels, nerves, bones, joints), especially 
in the setting of multimodality therapy. 
• For extremity sarcomas, the goal of surgery should be functional limb preservation, if possible, within the realm of an appropriate oncologic resection (“limb sparing 
surgery”).
• Radical excision/entire anatomic compartment resection is not routinely necessary.
• Prior to considering amputation, patients should be evaluated by a surgeon with expertise in the treatment of soft tissue sarcomas.
• Consideration for amputation to treat an extremity sarcoma should be made for patient preference or if gross total resection of the tumor is expected to render the limb 
nonfunctional.
Previous Unplanned Excision
• For patients who present with a previous unplanned surgical excision of an extremity, body wall, or head/neck sarcoma, staging imaging of the primary tumor site will 
help to determine the optimal management for local control.
• For gross residual tumor, follow EXTSARC-1.

Discussion
Previous Unplanned Excision (continued)
• For no gross residual tumor, but nononcologic close or microscopically positive margins, management of the primary tumor site will be the same as for “Failure to 
Obtain Oncologically Appropriate Margins” below.
For sarcomas at high risk for metastatic disease (stage III), systemic therapy should also be considered as per EXTSARC-3.
• Following unplanned excision, data suggest some increased risk of local recurrence thus supporting a combined local control approach with re-resection and RT, 
preferably preoperative.  
• For retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas, unplanned spillage of microscopic tumor into the free intra-abdominal cavity is associated with an increased risk for 
future sarcomatosis. See R1 and R2 management algorithms in RETSARC-3.
Surgical Technique
• Ideally, any prior surgical or biopsy site potentially contaminated with microscopic tumor should be excised en bloc with the definitive surgical resection specimen if 
technically possible with acceptable morbidity. 
En bloc resection of a prior core needle biopsy tract in the setting of either preoperative or planned postoperative RT is less critical for local control. 
• Dissection should be through grossly normal tissue planes uncontaminated by tumor. 
• If the tumor is close to or displaces major vessels or nerves, these do not need to be resected if the adventitia or perineurium is removed and the underlying 
neurovascular structures are not involved with gross tumor.
• Surgical clips may be placed to mark the periphery of the surgical field and other relevant structures to help guide potential future RT. 
• If closed suction drainage is used, the drains should exit the skin close to the edge of the surgical incision (in case re-resection or radiation is indicated).
• Clinical surgical margins at the time of resection should be documented by the surgeon.
• For certain histologies that are highly infiltrative, such as myxofibrosarcoma and DFSP, staged resections (with temporary wound coverage) to obtain negative 
pathologic margins prior to definitive closure/reconstruction can reduce the risk for local recurrence.3,4,5
Failure to Obtain Oncologically Appropriate Margins (Extremity, Truncal, Head/Neck)
• If soft tissue surgical resection margins other than bone, nerve, or major blood vessels are unexpectedly positive on final pathology (or following an unplanned 
excision), then surgical re-resection to obtain negative margins should strongly be considered if technically possible with minimal adverse impact on functionality. 
• If there is a close/microscopically positive soft tissue margin that cannot be re-resected, then adjuvant RT should be considered based upon the risk for as well as the 
potential morbidity of a future local recurrence. 
For patients with ALT/WDLPS, observation is recommended for focally positive margins if re-resection, in the event of recurrence, would not be unduly morbid. RT is 
reserved for selected patients with recurrent or deeply infiltrative primary lesions with a risk of local recurrence, depending on the tumor location and patient’s age.
On a case-by-case basis, close observation is also a management option for some other Stage IA sarcoma histologies.
• Re-resection for either a planned or unplanned excision with close/microscopically positive margins on critical structures (bone, nerve, or major blood vessels) is 
usually not technically possible without significant morbidity. Therefore, strong considerations should be given to adjuvant RT (unless preoperative RT was already 
administered).
Failure to Obtain Oncologically Appropriate Margins (Retroperitoneal/Intra-abdominal)
• Given the close proximity of retroperitoneal sarcomas to critical structures, close or microscopically positive margins are not uncommon. However,  
re-resection and/or adjuvant RT are not typically considered in this situation (RETSARC-3).

SARC-D 
2 OF 4

Discussion
Prehabilitation/Rehabilitation
• Surgery for extremity soft tissue sarcomas can often result in physical disability, especially following amputation. Resection of truncal and intra-
abdominal/retroperitoneal sarcomas can also adversely impact major organ systems (including cardiopulmonary function), nutrition, and general 
performance status. Therefore, when significant postoperative dysfunction is anticipated, strong consideration should be given to both outpatient 
prehabilitation and rehabilitation to optimize functional outcomes and the patient’s quality of life.
Prehabilitation
• All patients should undergo a thorough preoperative assessment of medical comorbidities, physical endurance/frailty, nutritional status, and the 
specifics of the postoperative home environment/psychological support.
• Prior to amputation or major limb-sparing surgery, a formal PM&R consultation should be offered to provide education regarding the expected functional 
outcomes following the planned surgery, set up postoperative therapy goals, demonstrate postoperative exercises, and establish care for longitudinal 
follow-up.
• Multimodality prehabilitation (exercise training, nutritional supplementation, and anxiety-coping techniques) is associated with greater improvements in 
functional capacity and a lower risk for postoperative complications following major abdominal surgery.6 Therefore, this should be strongly considered 
to optimize patients with comorbidities undergoing retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcoma resection.
• Although there might not be adequate time for extensive prehabilitation if surgery is being performed immediately, most soft tissue sarcoma patients 
receiving neoadjuvant chemotherapy and/or preoperative radiation will have several months before surgery to undergo a formal  
prehabilitation assessment/regimen.
Rehabilitation 
• In the immediate postoperative period, all patients should undergo a physical functionality and home environment evaluation (typically by a physical 
therapist) to ensure that they can be safely discharged home. If further inpatient rehabilitation is needed, a PM&R physician and occupational therapist 
should also evaluate the patient.
• Close communication between the orthopedic/surgical oncologist and the rehabilitation team is essential, including postoperative physical restrictions, 
anticipated concerns, and a coordinated review of the rehabilitation plan. This is especially true for more complex limb-sparing surgeries (eg, oncologic 
proximal humerus replacement, proximal tibia replacement, internal hemipelvectomy) that require adequate scar  
tissue formation for functional joint recovery.

SARC-D

Rehabilitation (continued)
• The rehabilitation plan should consider oncology treatment-related side effects and comorbidities that could potentially impact recovery such as 
lymphedema, systemic therapy-induced neuropathy and fatigue, radiation fibrosis, and impaired bone healing.
• When possible, a more complex rehabilitation plan should be overseen by a PM&R physician, who can prescribe medications, order and  
interpret diagnostic tests, and oversee/coordinate therapies.
• Pain management should be integrated into the rehabilitation plan as early as possible, especially for neuropathic pain or phantom limb sensation/pain 
following amputation. Interventions can include mirror therapy, motor imagery, massage, oral and topical analgesics, coping strategies, and patient 
education.
• The rehabilitation plan should also address any psychological concerns (eg, distress associated with the surgery/decreased physical ability, body 
image issues) and include referrals to appropriate mental health professionals, if appropriate. All patients should be offered  
participation in peer support groups.

SARC-E
1 OF 6
Radiation Therapy Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma of Extremity/Body Wall/Head and Neck1,2
Neoadjuvant RT
• The Panel has expressed a general preference for preoperative over postoperative radiotherapy.3  
• Potential benefits of preoperative radiation include lower total radiation dose, shorter treatment time, smaller field sizes, potential for reduced late 
toxicities (ie, fibrosis, edema, joint stiffness), and potential for tumor downstaging. Potential disadvantages include increased frequency of early wound 
healing complications, particularly for lower extremity tumors.4,5 
• A preoperative dose of 50 to 50.4 Gy external beam RT (EBRT) (1.8–2 Gy per fraction) is recommended.6-9  
• Radiation should be delivered using the most appropriate technique that will cover the target volume while maintaining dose constraints to normal 
tissues (which may include bone, lymphatics, and soft tissue). These techniques may include IMRT (preferred), or electron beam therapy, 3D conformal 
radiation therapy, or (in challenging anatomic locations) proton beam therapy. These treatments should be delivered with daily image guidance to 
ensure adequate targeting of the tumor and sparing of adjacent normal tissue.10-12
• If an R1 or R2 resection is anticipated, placement of clips in areas at high risk for recurrence is encouraged. 
• A dose reduction to 36 Gy for myxoid liposarcoma can be considered.13
• Conventionally fractionated RT remains the standard of care. A moderately hypofractionated preoperative regimen has demonstrated acceptable early 
local control, but long-term local control and toxicity data are not yet available. A hypofractionated regimen may be appropriate for select patients who 
might otherwise be unable to receive a standard course of preoperative radiotherapy.14-17
a These guidelines are intended to treat the adult population. For adolescent and young adult patients, see Oncology Guidelines for Adolescent and Young Adult (AYA) 
Oncology.

Discussion
Radiation Therapy Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma of Extremity/Body Wall/Head and Neck1,2
Adjuvant RT
• Potential benefits of postoperative radiotherapy include ability to review final surgical pathology including margin status and lower rate of wound healing 
complications (especially in the lower extremity). Potential disadvantages include larger field sizes, higher radiation doses, and potential for increased risk of 
late toxicities (ie, fibrosis, edema, joint stiffness).4,5
• For positive margins after preoperative radiation and surgery, consider observation or RT boost in select situations.
There are data to suggest that some patients with positive margins following neoadjuvant RT such as those with low-grade WDLPS and a focally, “planned” 
positive margin on an anatomically fixed critical structure may have acceptable local control without a boost.18
There are data to suggest that delivery of a boost for positive margins does not improve local control. Since delivery of an adjuvant RT boost does not clearly 
add benefit, the decision should be individualized and the potential toxicities should be carefully considered.19,20
If adjuvant boost radiation for a positive margin is felt to be appropriate, an additional 14 to 20 Gy can be considered with fractionated EBRT or 
brachytherapy.21
• Postoperative dose guidelines (total doses should always be determined by normal tissue tolerance):
EBRT (50–50.4 Gy; 1.8–2.0 Gy per fraction) to larger volume followed by a 10- to 16-Gy boost to reduced volume encompassing the tumor bed (final dose of 
60 Gy for R0 and 66 Gy for R1 resection margins)
Brachytherapy ± EBRT 
 ◊Positive margins: Low dose-rate (16–20 Gy) or high dose-rate equivalent brachytherapy (14–16 Gy) + 50 Gy EBRT
 ◊Negative margins: Low dose-rate (45 Gy) or high dose-rate equivalent brachytherapy (36 Gy in 3.6 Gy BID over 10 fractions in 5 days) 
 
Definitive RT for Unresectable Disease22  
• Long-term local control from radiation alone may be inferior to multimodality therapy, but this is a reasonable option for nonsurgical candidates.
• Doses are based on the clinical scenario, histology, and anatomic location prioritizing nearby critical structures.
• Doses to the initial volume should be 50 Gy with a boost of at least 63 Gy; however, higher doses of 70 to 80 Gy can be considered, based on tolerance of 
normal structures.
• Alternative hypofractionated regimens may be considered.23-25
Radiation Therapy Guidelines for Desmoid Tumors
• Definitive RT is an appropriate treatment option primarily for patients with non-mesenteric desmoid tumors. Treatment is often reserved for patients who cannot 
tolerate or progress through systemic therapy and where surgery would be too morbid or would result in positive margins.
The recommended dose of definitive RT is 56 Gy in 28 fractions.26  
• Treat similarly to high-grade soft tissue sarcoma, keeping in mind the infiltrative nature of desmoid tumors.27-29
• Although no prospective data exists, there appears to be a benefit to definitive and postoperative radiation in doses ≥50 to 56 Gy, administered in standard 1.8 
to 2 Gy/fraction postoperatively.30-32

Radiation Therapy Guidelines for Retroperitoneal/Intra-Abdominal Sarcoma
• When EBRT is used, treatment planning with IMRT, image-guided RT (IGRT), and/or proton beam therapy should be used to improve the therapeutic 
ratio.10,11
Neoadjuvant RT30-32
• Neoadjuvant RT for retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcomas can be considered in selected patients at high risk for local recurrence.
• If neoadjuvant RT is deemed to be appropriate for a patient, the following dose guidelines are recommended:
50 to 50.4 Gy EBRT (1.8–2.0 Gy per fraction)
Consider IORT boost for known or suspected positive margins at the time of surgery
 ◊10–12.5 Gy for microscopically positive disease
 ◊15 Gy for gross disease
In experienced centers only: 45 to 50 Gy in 25 to 28 fractions to entire clinical target volume (CTV) with dose-painted simultaneous integrated boost 
(SIB) to total dose of 57.5 Gy in 25 fractions to the high-risk retroperitoneal margin jointly defined by the surgeon and radiation oncologist (no boost 
after surgery)33
Adjuvant RT34-36
• Adjuvant RT following surgery is discouraged for retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcoma. If RT is not given prior to surgical resection, consider follow-
up with possible neoadjuvant RT at time of localized recurrence. See SARC-D.
Radiation Therapy Guidelines for Borderline/Malignant Phyllodes Tumor
• Data supports postoperative radiation in certain scenarios. General sarcoma dose guidelines are often utilized with 50–60 Gy administered in standard 
1.8–2 Gy/fraction postoperatively.37,38
• The timing of preoperative versus postoperative radiation needs to be determined in tandem with the surgeon and multidisciplinary team. Factors that 
may inform this decision include tumor size, proximity to skin and chest wall, the planned extent of surgery, and reconstructive considerations.
• There are no data supporting the use of hypofractionated radiation regimens in phyllodes tumors therefore conventionally fractionated whole breast/
post-mastectomy radiation is recommended, consistent with the dosing used for soft tissue sarcomas.

SARC-E
4 OF 6

General Treatment Guidelines for Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy (SBRT)39-45  
• SBRT delivers ablative doses of radiation to tumors using specialized patient immobilization, treatment planning, and image guidance to ensure a high 
degree of precision and facilitate protection of adjacent normal tissues.  
• SBRT is commonly used to treat metastatic sarcomas involving the lungs, liver, bone, lymph nodes, and other anatomic sites, but could also be used 
for select primary tumors.
• Dose and fractionation should be determined by an experienced radiation oncologist based on normal tissue constraints.
• Patient selection:
Multidisciplinary discussion can help identify patients that may benefit from SBRT.
Patients with oligometastatic disease.
Patients with oligoprogressive disease with other sites of disease stable or controlled by systemic therapy.
Patients who require treatment to a tumor within a previously irradiated field.
a These guidelines are intended to treat the adult population. For adolescent and young adult patients, see Oncology Guidelines for Adolescent and Young Adult (AYA) 
Oncology.
References on SARC-E 5 of 6
Palliative RT
• Palliative radiation can improve symptoms from unresectable or metastatic disease. Radiation dose and fractionation should be determined based 
on normal tissue constraints, performance status, patient prognosis, and prior treatment history. Patients with limited volume metastatic disease and 
an excellent performance status may benefit from dose escalation through IMRT or SBRT. Patients who have previously received irradiation to the 
treatment site may benefit from IMRT or SBRT to improve the safety of reirradiation.

Table of Contents
Discussion

SARC-F 
1 OF 3
Catheter-Directed Therapies
• In appropriately selected patients with liver-dominant metastatic disease, surgical resection of hepatic metastases can be associated with long-term 
survival benefit.1-3
• Catheter-directed therapies allow minimally invasive treatment of liver disease in select patients, including those unable to tolerate surgical resection or 
lesions not amenable to surgery.
• Intra-arterial therapies produce cell death by inducing ischemia and/or locally delivering cytotoxic agents or radiation to hepatic tumors. Specific intra-
arterial therapies include transarterial (bland) embolization (TAE), transarterial chemoembolization (TACE), and transarterial radioembolization (TARE). 
These therapies have been shown to be safe treatments for progressive disease with high rates of local disease control and overall survival.4-6
Transarterial Embolization (TAE)
 ◊TAE involves delivery of embolic agents within hepatic arteries supplying liver tumors with the goal of vessel stasis. 
 ◊TAE could be considered for treatment of hepatic sarcoma metastases in patients with unresectable liver disease or those who have disease that 
progresses despite conventional therapy.4
Transarterial Chemoembolization (TACE)
 ◊TACE consists of conventional TACE (cTACE) and drug-eluting bead (DEB)-TACE. cTACE involves targeted infusion of chemotherapeutic 
medications in addition to embolic agents and lipiodol into the tumoral blood supply while DEB-TACE drug delivery is through embolic beads loaded 
with chemotherapeutic medication.
 ◊In surgically unresectable liver disease, TACE has been shown to increase overall survival in patients with sarcoma who have liver metastases.7,8
Transarterial Radioembolization (TARE)
 ◊TARE utilizes beta particle emitting microspheres by yttrium-90 decay to induce tumoricidal effects through local brachytherapy. TARE can be 
performed with either glass or resin microspheres.9,10 
• Patient selection
Multidisciplinary discussion should be performed to identify candidates who may benefit from catheter-directed therapies.
Unresectable liver-dominant metastases or patients with medical comorbidities prohibiting surgical resection can be considered for catheter-directed 
therapies.
Patients whose disease progresses despite conventional therapies could also be considered for transarterial treatments. 
Absolute contraindications to catheter-directed therapies are few, but include:
 ◊Uncorrectable coagulopathy
 ◊Active infection in the planned treatment area
 ◊Decompensated liver failure (relative based on treatment approach)

SARC-F 
2 OF 3
Ablation 
• Tumor ablation involves application of FDA-approved thermal or non-thermal device therapies to a tumor to achieve cell death. 
• Tumor ablation techniques include thermal and non-thermal approaches. 
Ablation modality should be based on tumor size, location, and adjacent critical structures to optimize treatment effect while limiting potential adverse 
events. Choice of treatment modality may also take into account institutional experience and technique availability. 
• Patient selection
Multidisciplinary discussion should be considered to identify candidates who may benefit from ablative therapies.
Unresectable metastases or patients with medical comorbidities prohibiting surgical resection could be considered for image-guided ablation.
Patients whose disease progresses despite conventional therapies could also be considered for ablation.
Ablation after stability on chemotherapy can serve as a well-tolerated maintenance therapy with significant progression-free survival and systemic 
therapy-free intervals.11
Ablation for patients with painful musculoskeletal metastases could be considered for pain palliation and may be combined with cement augmentation, 
osteosynthesis, or internal fixation in the event of osseous destruction or increased fracture risk, especially in weight-bearing bones.
Absolute contraindications to image-guided ablation are few, but include:
 ◊ Uncorrectable coagulopathy
 ◊ Active infection in the planned treatment area
 ◊Inability to displace or protect adjacent critical structures (relative based on risk-benefit discussion)
• Specific considerations in ablation of metastatic disease
Liver metastases
 ◊Local ablative treatment is associated with improved overall survival in patients with oligometastatic disease.12
Lung metastases
 ◊Percutaneous thermal ablation may be considered in the treatment of sarcoma lung metastases given high rates of local tumor control.13,14
Musculoskeletal metastases
 ◊Percutaneous ablation is an effective option for local tumor control in the setting of oligometastatic disease and pain palliation of metastatic sarcomas 
within the musculoskeletal system.15
• Specific considerations of ablation in desmoid tumors
Ablation can provide good local control and favorable clinical response rates when used as active therapy for progressive, morbid, or symptomatic 
desmoids.16-26
Cryoablation is the most commonly utilized ablation modality.27 
DEB chemoembolization in desmoid tumors can be utilized for selective extra-abdominal desmoid tumors if cryoablation is not technically possible.28

FOOTNOTES
SYSTEMIC THERAPY AGENTS AND REGIMENS WITH ACTIVITY IN SOFT TISSUE SARCOMA SUBTYPES AND  
AGGRESSIVE SOFT TISSUE NEOPLASMS
a Prior to the initiation of therapy, all patients should be evaluated and treated by a multidisciplinary team with expertise and experience in sarcoma. 
b For uterine sarcomas, see the Oncology Guidelines for Uterine Neoplasms.
c Excluding histologies that are insensitive to cytotoxic chemotherapy including, but not limited to, ASPS, extremity ALT/WDLPS, extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma, 
and clear cell sarcomas. These regimens are appropriate for many STS histologies, but refer to other sections for additional histology-specific recommendations.
d Dexrazoxane may be added as a cardioprotectant for the prevention of cardiotoxicity in patients planning to receive high-dose anthracyclines (eg, doxorubicin >250 
mg/m2). Armenian SH, et al. J Clin Oncol 2017;35:893-911.
e Anthracycline-based regimens are preferred in the neoadjuvant and adjuvant settings.
f Regimens appropriate for pleomorphic RMS.
g See SARC-G 6 of 14 for additional treatment options for dedifferentiated liposarcoma with or without concurrent well-differentiated liposarcoma.
h Not intended for neoadjuvant or adjuvant therapy of nonmetastatic disease. Not recommended for angiosarcoma or pleomorphic RMS.
i Not intended for adjuvant therapy of nonmetastatic disease.
j Recommended only for palliative therapy.
k For non-adipocytic sarcoma.
l Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy is not approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab; however, for nivolumab monotherapy, nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy 
subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV 
nivolumab.
m For the treatment of patients with unresectable or metastatic tumor mutational burden-high (TMB-H) (≥10 mut/Mb) tumors, as determined by an FDA-approved test, 
that have progressed following prior treatment and who have no satisfactory alternative treatment options.
n For the treatment of unresectable or metastatic MSI-H or mismatch repair deficient (dMMR) solid tumors as determined by an FDA-approved test, that have 
progressed following prior treatment and who have no satisfactory alternative treatment options.
o Optimal duration of TKI therapy has not been established. Discontinuation of TKI therapy can be considered (with careful monitoring) in patients with stable disease. 
p For patients with intermediate-risk disease, consider maintenance therapy with vinorelbine and cyclophosphamide for 6 months.
q FDA approved for the treatment of adult and pediatric patients ≥2 years of age with unresectable or metastatic ASPS.
r Atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV atezolizumab. Atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs has different dosing and 
administration instructions compared to IV atezolizumab.
s An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for bevacizumab.
t Single-agent therapy for the treatment of unresectable WDLPS or dedifferentiated liposarcoma.
u Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph 
has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV pembrolizumab.

header: PRINCIPLES OF CANCER RISK ASSESSMENT AND COUNSELING
content:
SARC-H
• See the following for a thorough discussion of how and when to consider testing, important elements of pre-test counseling, points to consider when 
using multigene testing, how tumor testing can inform germline testing, important elements in post-test counseling, and the importance of family 
communication:
Principles of Cancer Risk Assessment and Counseling (Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, 
and Prostate [EVAL-A])
• For pedigree development, see Pedigree: First-, Second-, and Third-Degree Relatives of Proband (Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk 
Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate [EVAL-B])
• When to consider genetic testing for inherited soft tissue sarcomas:
For Li-Fraumeni syndrome, see Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate
For HNPCC (or Lynch syndrome) and FAP/attenuated FAP (AFAP) for desmoid tumors, see Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk 
Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric
For patients with personal/family history suggestive of other cancer predisposition syndromes, consider further genetics assessment.

Version 3.2026, 03/12/26 © 2026 Oncology Guidelines® (Oncology Guidelines®), All rights reserved. Oncology Guidelines® and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) Staging System for Soft Tissue Sarcoma of the Head and Neck (8th ed, 2017)
Definitions for T, N, M
T
Primary Tumor
TX
Primary tumor cannot be assessed
T1
Tumor ≤2 cm
T2
Tumor >2 cm to ≤4 cm
T3
Tumor >4 cm
T4
Tumor with invasion of adjoining structures
T4a
Tumor with orbital invasion, skull base/dural invasion, invasion of 
central compartment viscera, involvement of facial skeleton, or 
invasion of pterygoid muscles
T4b
Tumor with brain parenchymal invasion, carotid artery encasement, 
prevertebral muscle invasion, or central nervous system 
involvement via perineural spread
N
Regional Lymph Nodes
N0
No regional lymph node metastasis or unknown lymph node status
N1
Regional lymph node metastasis
M
Distant Metastasis
M0
No distant metastasis
M1
Distant metastasis
G
Definition of Grade 
FNCLCC Histologic Grade - see Histologic Grade (G)
GX
Grade cannot be assessed
G1
Total differentiation, mitotic count and necrosis score of 2 or 3
G2
Total differentiation, mitotic count and necrosis score of 4 or 5
G3
Total differentiation, mitotic count and necrosis score of 6, 7, or 8
Anatomic Stage/Prognostic Groups
This is a new classification that needs data collection before defining a stage 
grouping for head and neck sarcomas.
Histologic Grade (G)
The FNCLCC grade is determined by three parameters: differentiation, mitotic 
activity, and extent of necrosis. Each parameter is scored as follows: differentiation 
(1-3), mitotic activity (1-3), and necrosis (0-2). The scores are added to determine 
the grade.
Tumor Differentiation
1
Sarcomas closely resembling normal adult mesenchymal tissue (e.g., low-
grade leiomyosarcoma)
2
Sarcomas for which histologic typing is certain (e.g., myxoid/round cell 
liposarcoma)
3
Embryonal and undifferentiated sarcomas, sarcomas of doubtful type, 
synovial sarcomas, soft tissue osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma/primitive 
neuroectodermal tumor (PNET) of soft tissue
Mitotic Count
In the most mitotically active area of the sarcoma, 10 successive high-power fields 
(HPF; one HPF at 400× magnification = 0.1734 mm2) are assessed using a 40× 
objective.
1
0-9 mitoses per 10 HPF
2
10-19 mitoses per 10 HPF
3
≥20 mitoses per 10 HPF
Tumor Necrosis
Evaluated on gross examination and validated with histologic sections.
0
No necrosis
1
<50% tumor necrosis
2
≥50% tumor necrosis
Histopathologic Type

CAT-1
Oncology Guidelines Categories of Evidence and Consensus
Category 1
Based upon high-level evidence (≥1 randomized phase 3 trials or high-quality, robust meta-analyses), there is 
uniform Oncology Guidelines consensus (≥85% support of the Panel) that the intervention is appropriate.
Category 2A
Based upon lower-level evidence, there is uniform Oncology Guidelines consensus (≥85% support of the Panel) that the 
intervention is appropriate.
Category 2B
Based upon lower-level evidence, there is Oncology Guidelines consensus (≥50%, but <85% support of the Panel) that the 
intervention is appropriate.
Category 3
Based upon any level of evidence, there is major Oncology Guidelines disagreement that the intervention is appropriate. 
All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Categories of Preference
Preferred
Interventions that are based on superior efficacy, safety, and evidence; and, when appropriate, 
affordability.
Other recommended
Other interventions that may be somewhat less efficacious, more toxic, or based on less mature data; 
or significantly less affordable for similar outcomes.
Useful in certain 
circumstances
Other interventions that may be used for selected patient populations (defined with recommendation).
All recommendations are considered appropriate.

MS-2
Oncology Guidelines Version 3.2026 
Soft Tissue Sarcoma
Overview
Sarcomas are a rare and heterogeneous group of solid tumors of 
mesenchymal origin accounting for only 1% of all adult malignancies and 
15% of childhood malignancies. They can be divided broadly into:
• 
Sarcomas of soft tissues (including fat, muscle, nerve and nerve 
sheath, blood vessels, and other connective tissues) and  
• 
Sarcomas of bone
In 2022, an estimated 13,190 people will be diagnosed with soft tissue 
sarcoma (STS) in the United States, with approximately 5130 deaths.1 
The true incidence of STS is underestimated, especially because a large 
proportion of patients with gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) may not 
have been included in tumor registry databases before 2001. Prior 
radiation therapy (RT) to the affected area is a risk factor for the 
development of STS.2-4 Other risk factors that are associated with the 
development of STS include various chemicals (eg, herbicides, such as 
agent orange) as well as genetic syndromes (eg, Li-Fraumeni syndrome, 
neurofibromatosis).5 More than 50 different histologic subtypes of STS 
have been identified.  STS most commonly metastasizes to the lungs; 
tumors arising in the abdominal cavity more commonly metastasize to the 
liver and peritoneum.
The Oncology Guidelines® for Soft Tissue Sarcoma address the management 
of STS in adult patients from the perspective of the following disease 
subtypes:
• 
STS of extremity, superficial/trunk, or head and neck 
• 
Retroperitoneal or intra-abdominal STS 
• 
Desmoid tumors (aggressive fibromatoses) 
• 
Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS)
The anatomic site of the primary disease represents an important variable 
that influences treatment and outcome. Extremities (43%), the trunk 
(10%), visceral (19%), retroperitoneum (15%), or head and neck (9%) are 
the most common primary sites.6 Desmoid tumors, or aggressive 
fibromatosis (AF), are a unique soft tissue tumor subtype that is 
characterized by local infiltration rather than distant metastasis. RMS is 
the most common STS of children and adolescents, and is less common 
in adults.
Prior to initiation of treatment, all patients should be evaluated and 
managed by a multidisciplinary team with extensive expertise and 
experience in the treatment of STS.7 Because STS is rare and often 
complex, adherence to evidence-based recommendations is particularly 
important. Analysis of data from 15,957 patients with STS in the National 
Cancer Database (NCDB) showed that Oncology Guidelines-adherent 
treatment was associated with improved survival outcomes.8
Literature Search Criteria and Guidelines Update 
Methodology
Prior to the update of this version of the Oncology Guidelines for Soft Tissue 
Sarcoma, an electronic search of the PubMed database was performed to 
obtain key literature in STS, using the following search terms: soft tissue 
sarcoma OR desmoid OR aggressive fibromatosis OR 
rhabdomyosarcoma OR *sarcoma. The PubMed database was chosen as 
it remains the most widely used resource for medical literature and 
indexes only peer-reviewed biomedical literature.
The search results were narrowed by selecting studies in humans 
published in English. Results were confined to the following article types: 
Clinical Study; Clinical Trial; Guideline; Randomized Controlled Trial; 
Meta-Analysis; Systematic Reviews; and Validation Studies.
